examples.title: Algorithm: Structure and Union Declaration and Access
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how to declare structures and unions, access members using dot and arrow operators, and understand memory sharing in unions
examples.code:
ALGORITHM StructureOperations
// Demonstrates structure declaration, initialization, and member access

BEGIN
 // 1. Declare a Structure
 DECLARE STRUCT Student
 DECLARE INTEGER usn
 DECLARE STRING name[50]
 DECLARE REAL percentage
 END STRUCT

 // 2. Declare Structure Variables
 DECLARE Student s1, s2
 DECLARE Student *ptr

 // 3. Initialize Structure
 s1.usn ← 101
 s1.name ← "Rahul"
 s1.percentage ← 85.5

 // 4. Access Members using Dot Operator
 PRINT s1.usn
 PRINT s1.name

 // 5. Access Members using Arrow Operator (via pointer)
 ptr ← ADDRESS_OF(s1)
 PRINT ptr->usn
 // Equivalent to: (*ptr).usn

 // 6. Nested Structure Declaration
 DECLARE STRUCT Date
 DECLARE INTEGER day
 DECLARE INTEGER month
 DECLARE INTEGER year
 END STRUCT

 DECLARE STRUCT Employee
 DECLARE INTEGER id
 DECLARE STRING name[50]
 DECLARE Date dob // Nested structure
 END STRUCT

 DECLARE Employee e1
 e1.dob.day ← 15
 e1.dob.month ← 8
 e1.dob.year ← 2004

 // 7. Array of Structures
 DECLARE Student class[3]
 class[0].usn ← 101
 class[1].usn ← 102

 // 8. Self-Referential Structure (for linked lists)
 DECLARE STRUCT Node
 DECLARE INTEGER data
 DECLARE Node *next // Pointer to same type
 END STRUCT

 DECLARE Node *head
 head ← ALLOCATE(sizeof(Node))
 head->data ← 10
 head->next ← NULL

 // 9. Union Declaration and Behavior
 DECLARE UNION Data
 DECLARE INTEGER i
 DECLARE REAL f
 DECLARE CHARACTER c
 END UNION

 DECLARE Data d
 d.i ← 65 // Store integer
 PRINT d.i // Output: 65
 PRINT d.c // Output: 'A' (same memory as int)
 d.f ← 3.14 // Overwrites memory
 PRINT d.f // Output: 3.14
 PRINT d.i // Output: GARBAGE (overwritten by float)

END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode covers all key concepts: structure declaration and member access using dot (.) and arrow (->) operators, nested structures for related data grouping, arrays of structures for collections, self-referential structures for linked lists, and unions showing shared memory behavior where writing to one member overwrites others.

examples.title: Python: Structure and Union Concepts
examples.description: Python implementation showing equivalent concepts to C structures using classes and demonstrating union-like behavior with memory sharing
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Python equivalent to C Structures and Unions
Using classes to demonstrate similar concepts
"""

from ctypes import Structure, Union, c_int, c_float, c_char
import sys

# ============================================
# STRUCTURES IN PYTHON (using ctypes or classes)
# ============================================

# Method 1: Using Python Classes (recommended for Pythonic code)
class Student:
 """Class equivalent to C struct Student"""
 def __init__(self, usn=0, name="", percentage=0.0):
 self.usn = usn
 self.name = name
 self.percentage = percentage
 
 def display(self):
 print(f"USN: {self.usn}")
 print(f"Name: {self.name}")
 print(f"Percentage: {self.percentage:.2f}")


# Method 2: Using ctypes (direct C structure equivalent)
class CStudent(Structure):
 _fields_ = [("usn", c_int),
 ("name", c_char * 50),
 ("percentage", c_float)]


# ============================================
# NESTED STRUCTURES
# ============================================

class Date:
 """Nested structure equivalent"""
 def __init__(self, day=0, month=0, year=0):
 self.day = day
 self.month = month
 self.year = year


class Employee:
 """Structure with nested structure"""
 def __init__(self, emp_id=0, name="", dob=None):
 self.emp_id = emp_id
 self.name = name
 self.dob = dob if dob else Date() # Nested structure


# ============================================
# ARRAY OF STRUCTURES
# ============================================

def array_of_structures():
 """Demonstrates array of structures equivalent"""
 students = [
 Student(101, "Rahul", 85.5),
 Student(102, "Priya", 91.2),
 Student(103, "Karthik", 78.9)
 ]
 
 print("\n--- Array of Structures (Student Records) ---")
 print(f"{'USN':<5} {'Name':<10} {'Percentage':<10}")
 print("-" * 25)
 for s in students:
 print(f"{s.usn:<5} {s.name:<10} {s.percentage:<10.2f}")


# ============================================
# SELF-REFERENTIAL STRUCTURE (Linked List)
# ============================================

class Node:
 """Self-referential structure for linked list"""
 def __init__(self, data=0):
 self.data = data
 self.next = None # Pointer to next Node


def create_linked_list():
 """Create and display a simple linked list"""
 # Create nodes
 head = Node(10)
 second = Node(20)
 third = Node(30)
 
 # Link nodes (equivalent to ->next in C)
 head.next = second
 second.next = third
 third.next = None # End of list
 
 # Traverse and print
 print("\n--- Linked List (Self-Referential Structure) ---")
 current = head
 while current:
 print(f"{current.data} -> ", end="")
 current = current.next
 print("NULL")


# ============================================
# UNION IN PYTHON (using ctypes)
# ============================================

class CUnionData(Union):
 """Union equivalent using ctypes - all fields share same memory"""
 _fields_ = [("i", c_int),
 ("f", c_float),
 ("c", c_char)]


def union_demo():
 """Demonstrate union behavior - shared memory"""
 print("\n--- Union Demo (Shared Memory) ---")
 
 d = CUnionData()
 
 # Store integer value
 d.i = 65
 print(f"After d.i = 65:")
 print(f" d.i = {d.i}") # 65
 print(f" d.c = {d.c}") # 'A' (ASCII 65)
 
 # Store float - overwrites integer memory
 d.f = 3.14
 print(f"\nAfter d.f = 3.14:")
 print(f" d.f = {d.f}") # 3.14
 print(f" d.i = {d.i}") # Garbage value (overwritten!)
 
 print(f"\nUnion size: {sizeof(d)} bytes (size of largest member: int=4 bytes)")


# ============================================
# MAIN FUNCTION
# ============================================

def main():
 print("=" * 50)
 print("STRUCTURES AND UNIONS IN PYTHON")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 # Basic Structure
 s1 = Student(101, "Rahul", 85.5)
 print("\n--- Basic Structure ---")
 s1.display()
 
 # Nested Structure
 emp = Employee(501, "John", Date(15, 8, 2004))
 print("\n--- Nested Structure ---")
 print(f"Employee: {emp.name}, DOB: {emp.dob.day}/{emp.dob.month}/{emp.dob.year}")
 
 # Array of Structures
 array_of_structures()
 
 # Self-Referential Structure (Linked List)
 create_linked_list()
 
 # Union
 union_demo()
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 50)
 print("KEY DIFFERENCES FROM C:")
 print("- Python uses classes instead of struct keyword")
 print("- Memory management is automatic (garbage collection)")
 print("- Unions require ctypes for exact C behavior")
 print("=" * 50)


if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates equivalent concepts to C structures using classes. It shows: basic structure with __init__, nested structures, arrays of structures, self-referential structures (linked list nodes), and unions using ctypes to replicate C's shared memory behavior. The union demo explicitly shows how writing one member overwrites others due to shared memory.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for linked list traversal, O(1) for all other operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing n students, O(1) for basic operations

examples.title: C: Comprehensive Structures and Unions
examples.description: Complete C implementation covering structure declaration, typedef, nested structures, arrays of structures, self-referential structures (linked list), and unions with shared memory demonstration
examples.explanation: This comprehensive C implementation covers all key topics: basic structure declaration with dot and arrow operators, typedef for cleaner syntax, nested structures for hierarchical data, arrays of structures for collections, self-referential structures for linked lists, unions demonstrating shared memory behavior, and a detailed comparison between structures and unions showing memory allocation differences.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for basic operations, O(n) for array traversal and linked list traversal
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for structure variables, O(n) for arrays and linked lists

examples.title: C: Pointers to Structures and Function Parameters
examples.description: Demonstrates passing structures to functions by value and by reference, arrow operator usage, and dynamic memory allocation for structures
examples.explanation: This implementation demonstrates crucial concepts for working with structures in C: creating and using pointers to structures, the arrow operator (->) as syntactic sugar for (*ptr).member, passing structures to functions by value vs by reference, returning structures from functions, dynamically allocating structures with malloc, using const with structure pointers, and accessing nested structure members through pointers.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for basic structures, O(n) for dynamically allocated structures
